hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits, highest of all sections) represents unenciphered or minimally enciphered natural language prose, while lower-entropy sections use a homophonic expansion layer that artificially reduces entropy by replacing single plaintext glyphs with multiple ciphertext glyphs.
evidence_for: Text-only section has highest entropy (3.9016 bits), closest to natural language baselines (Latin ~4.0, Italian ~3.95)
Homophonic substitution would reduce entropy in proportion to the number of homophones used per plaintext character
Zodiac section has lowest entropy (3.7149 bits) and is label-heavy, consistent with repetitive short labels encoded homophones
evidence_against: If text-only were unenciphered, word boundaries and word forms should resemble known medieval languages more closely
High hapax ratio (70.1%) conflicts with expectation that natural-language prose would show more word repetition
hypotheses.test: Compute mutual information between section-level entropy and average word length; if homophonic expansion is active, sections with lower entropy should show longer average word lengths due to ciphertext expansion
hypotheses.test_metric: Pearson correlation between per-section entropy and per-section mean word length
hypotheses.test_threshold: Correlation r < -0.6 (p < 0.05) would support homophonic expansion hypothesis; r near 0 would refute it
tests_remaining: Compute mutual information between section-level entropy and average word length; if homophonic expansion is active, sections with lower entropy should show longer average word lengths due to ciphertext expansion
hypotheses.reasoning: If lower-entropy sections use homophones, each plaintext character maps to more ciphertext glyphs, inflating word length while reducing per-glyph entropy. This predicts an inverse correlation between entropy and word length across sections that is directly testable.

hypotheses.claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is produced by a systematic suffix-agglutination cipher mechanism: a small closed vocabulary of roots (~500-800 types) is combined with a set of suffix glyphs drawn from the word-final constraint set {y, n, l, r, o}, generating surface forms that appear unique but share common roots.
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs {y, n, l, r, o} are highly constrained and concentrated, consistent with a small morphological suffix inventory
If roots recombine with ~5 suffixes each, a vocabulary of 800 roots produces ~4,000 surface forms, approximating the ~8,261 unique types observed
Zipf exponent 0.8946 (below 1.0) is consistent with a generative vocabulary with moderate root reuse
evidence_against: True suffix agglutination in natural languages typically produces lower hapax ratios than 70.1%
If suffixes are purely cipher artifacts, stripping them should reveal a Zipfian root distribution with exponent closer to 1.0; this has not been confirmed
hypotheses.test: Strip word-final glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} iteratively from each token; compute vocabulary size and hapax ratio of the resulting root corpus; compare Zipf exponent of root distribution to full-text Zipf exponent
hypotheses.test_metric: Hapax ratio of root corpus after suffix stripping; Zipf exponent of root frequency distribution
hypotheses.test_threshold: Root hapax ratio < 40% and Zipf exponent > 1.0 after stripping would strongly support systematic suffix agglutination; no significant change would refute it
tests_remaining: Strip word-final glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} iteratively from each token; compute vocabulary size and hapax ratio of the resulting root corpus; compare Zipf exponent of root distribution to full-text Zipf exponent
hypotheses.reasoning: If the hapax explosion is suffix-driven, removing the suffix layer should dramatically collapse vocabulary size and reveal a more naturalistic frequency distribution, directly narrowing whether the cipher operates at the morpheme or word level.

hypotheses.claim: The Currier A/B split encodes two different plaintext languages (e.g., Latin in Currier A sections, early Italian or Occitan in Currier B sections), with each language using the same substitution cipher key but differing in phonological inventory, producing measurable differences in bigram transition probabilities between the two corpora.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show consistent scribal and distributional differences across folios
Different underlying phonologies would produce different glyph bigram frequencies even under the same cipher key
Current belief [0.552] supports two different plaintext languages for A/B split
evidence_against: Same cipher key assumption is not established; A/B differences could reflect two different keys on one language
Currier A/B split correlates with scribal hand differences, so observed statistical differences may be stylistic rather than linguistic
hypotheses.test: Compute glyph bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A and Currier B corpora; calculate Jensen-Shannon divergence between the two matrices; compare to JSD between randomly sampled equal-size subcorpora of each Currier class
hypotheses.test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence between Currier A and B bigram transition matrices
hypotheses.test_threshold: JSD > 0.05 bits (and significantly exceeding within-class JSD from bootstrap resampling at p < 0.01) would support distinct underlying phonologies; JSD near within-class variance would refute it
tests_remaining: Compute glyph bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A and Currier B corpora; calculate Jensen-Shannon divergence between the two matrices; compare to JSD between randomly sampled equal-size subcorpora of each Currier class
hypotheses.reasoning: Two different source languages under the same cipher key would produce detectably different bigram statistics reflecting their phonological differences. This test distinguishes linguistic divergence from mere scribal style variation.

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits) and label-heavy structure reflect a fixed-vocabulary encoding in which each label token is drawn from a closed set of fewer than 50 distinct label templates, with high within-label glyph redundancy serving as a positional marker rather than encoding phonemic content.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has fewest folios (12) and words (1,297) relative to other sections, consistent with a label-only register
Lowest section entropy (3.7149 bits) suggests reduced glyph diversity, compatible with a closed label vocabulary
Astronomical section also has low entropy (3.7471 bits) and is similarly diagram-heavy, supporting a diagram-label encoding regime
evidence_against: If labels use a closed template set, type-token ratio for the zodiac section should be markedly lower than other sections; this is testable but not yet confirmed
Low entropy could alternatively reflect a denser homophonic encoding rather than a restricted vocabulary
hypotheses.test: Compute type-token ratio (unique words / total words) for each section; test whether zodiac and astronomical sections have significantly lower TTR than herbal, recipes, and text-only sections using a one-sided permutation test
hypotheses.test_metric: Type-token ratio per section; permutation test p-value comparing label-heavy vs. prose-heavy sections
hypotheses.test_threshold: Zodiac TTR < 0.30 and permutation p < 0.05 relative to herbal/recipes/text-only sections would confirm closed-vocabulary label encoding; TTR comparable to other sections would refute it
tests_remaining: Compute type-token ratio (unique words / total words) for each section; test whether zodiac and astronomical sections have significantly lower TTR than herbal, recipes, and text-only sections using a one-sided permutation test
hypotheses.reasoning: If zodiac labels are drawn from a closed template set, the section should show a markedly lower TTR than prose sections. Confirming this would establish a two-register model (label vs. prose) that constrains decipherment strategies differently for each section.

hypotheses.claim: The dominant word-initial glyph constraints {o, c, q, s, d} are artifacts of a cipher mechanism that encodes plaintext vowels (a, e, i, o, u) as these five glyphs in word-initial position, with each cipher glyph mapping to a single plaintext vowel, producing a detectable co-occurrence pattern between word-initial cipher glyphs and word-final glyphs that mirrors vowel-consonant harmony in Latin or Italian.
evidence_for: Word-initial glyph constraints are strongly concentrated in just 5 glyphs, consistent with a small vowel inventory (Latin has 5 vowels, Italian 7)
If initial glyphs encode vowels, words beginning with each initial glyph should share statistical properties (length, following bigram) traceable to the underlying vowel's phonological context
Current belief [0.624] supports positional glyph constraints reflecting cipher structure
evidence_against: Latin and Italian words do not begin with vowels at the frequency implied by the dominance of these 5 initial glyphs
The cipher could instead encode consonant classes or null prefixes rather than vowels
q is almost exclusively followed by o in the Voynich text, suggesting a digraph rather than an independent vowel encoding
hypotheses.test: For each of the 5 dominant word-initial glyphs, compute the distribution of immediately following glyphs (second-position bigrams); test whether these second-position distributions are mutually exclusive or overlapping using a chi-squared test of independence across the 5 initial-glyph classes
hypotheses.test_metric: Chi-squared statistic and CramÃ©r's V for association between word-initial glyph identity and second-position glyph identity
hypotheses.test_threshold: CramÃ©r's V > 0.4 with p < 0.001 would confirm that initial glyphs partition into distinct phonological classes (consistent with vowel encoding); V < 0.2 would suggest initial glyphs are positional markers without phonemic specificity
tests_remaining: For each of the 5 dominant word-initial glyphs, compute the distribution of immediately following glyphs (second-position bigrams); test whether these second-position distributions are mutually exclusive or overlapping using a chi-squared test of independence across the 5 initial-glyph classes
hypotheses.reasoning: If initial glyphs encode specific vowels, each should consistently co-occur with a predictable set of following consonant glyphs reflecting the phonotactics of the underlying language. This test can distinguish phonemically meaningful initial constraints from purely positional cipher artifacts.
